perturbation.original_text:
2. Confidentiality
2.1. Either party agrees that it shall ensure that it and its associated companies and their respective officers and employees shall keep confidential, both during and after the duration of the agreement, all information which:2.2. Consists of any of the commercial secrets of the other party; or2.3 Consists of any other information of a confidential nature belonging to, or connected with, the other party and its activities and clients, and designated by such party as confidential;2.4 And undertakes not to disclose any of such information to any third party without the prior written permission of the other party except as required by any applicable law or government regulation, and provided that each party may disclose the agreement on a confidential basis to its public accountants, attorneys and financial advisors and/or funders.2.5 Neither party shall be required to keep confidential any information which is, or becomes, publicly available, is independently developed by either party outside the scope of this agreement, or is rightfully obtained from third parties.
perturbation.changed_text:
7. Execution
This agreement is executed for and on behalf of:Mandarin Trading (Pty) LtdBy .................................................. In his / her capacity as a director of the company SIGNATORY AS WITNESSSigned at ........................................... On .....................................................The Business PartnerBy .................................................. In his / her capacity as a director of the company SIGNATORY AS WITNESSSigned at ........................................... On .....................................................

2. Confidentiality
2.1. Either party agrees that it shall ensure that it and its associated companies and their respective officers and employees shall keep confidential, both during and after the duration of the agreement, all information which:2.2. Consists of any of the commercial secrets of the other party; or2.3 Consists of any other information of a confidential nature belonging to, or connected with, the other party and its activities and clients, and designated by such party as confidential;2.4 And undertakes not to disclose any of such information to any third party without the prior written permission of the other party except as required by any applicable law or government regulation, and provided that each party may disclose the agreement on a confidential basis to its public accountants, attorneys and financial advisors and/or funders.2.5 Neither party shall be required to keep confidential any information which is, or becomes, publicly available, is independently developed by either party outside the scope of this agreement, or is rightfully obtained from third parties.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the confidentiality clause to be after the execution clause is problematic because confidentiality is a fundamental aspect of NDAs. Relocating this to the end of the document makes it difficult for the business partner to ascertain if this clause applies before they agree to the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African common law principles regarding contract interpretation and reasonable notice.
perturbation.law_citation: Durban's Water Wonderland (Pty) Ltd v Botha 1999 (1) SA 982 (SCA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Durban's Water Wonderland case (and related common law principles) emphasizes the importance of reasonable notice of contractual terms. Placing the confidentiality clause after the execution section of the agreement could be seen as failing to provide reasonable notice of this crucial term, potentially rendering it unenforceable, especially if the business partner was not explicitly made aware of the clause before signing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Phillips v Botha (591/96) [1998] ZASCA 105; 1999 (2) SA 555 (SCA); [1999] 1 All SA 524 (A) (26 November 1998) About SAFLII Databases Search Terms of Use RSS Feeds South Africa: Supreme Court of Appeal You are here: SAFLII >> Databases >> South Africa: Supreme Court of Appeal >> 1998 >> [1998] ZASCA 105 | Noteup | LawCite Phillips v Botha (591/96) [1998] ZASCA 105; 1999 (2) SA 555 (SCA); [1999] 1 All SA 524 (A) (26 November 1998) Download original files PDF format RTF format CASE NO: 591/96 WLD In the matter between: ANDREW LIONEL PHILLIPS Appellant and PIERRE BOTHA Respondent Coram : Hefer, Hoexter, Howie, Schutz JJ A et Ngoepe AJA Heard: 2 November 1998 D elivered : 26 November 1998 JUDGMENT 2 HOEXTRR,JA : This is a civil appeal. The appellant is the owner of a casino in Rivonia. The respondent is an attorney in Florida . Following upon the issue by the Attorney-General of the Witwatersrand of a certificate of nolle prosequi, the appellant in terms of sec 7(1) of Act 51 of 1977 ("the Act") instituted and conducted in the Regional Court for the Southern Transvaal Division a private prosecution for fraud against respondent. The respondent pleaded not guilty to the charge. The appellant himself and two other persons testified in support of the charge. At the close of the prosecution's case counsel for the respondent applied for the discharge of his client. The application was granted and the respondent was found not guilty and discharged. Against the order of the regional court the appellant appealed unsuccessfully to the Witwatersrand Local Division. The judgment of the Court a quo (delivered by HEHER J with the concurrence of M J STRYDOM J) has been reported : Phillips v Botha 1995(3) SA 3 948(W). With leave of the Chief Justice the appellant now appeals to this Court. The respondent has a current account ("his personal account") at the President Street , Johannesburg , branch of the Trust Bank. At the Midrand branch of the same bank the respondent's firm has an account ("the firm's account") Late in the evening
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that moving the confidentiality clause to the end of the agreement makes it difficult for the business partner to ascertain if this clause applies before they agree to the agreement. The Law Explanation references the Durban's Water Wonderland case which emphasizes the importance of reasonable notice of contractual terms. Moving the confidentiality clause after the execution section could be seen as failing to provide reasonable notice of this crucial term, potentially rendering it unenforceable. Therefore, a structural flaw creates a legal contradiction.
